is [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), primarily the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Slovenia "Catholic Church in Slovenia"), which is the largest [Christian denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") in the country. Other Christian groups having significant followings in the country include [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") and [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") ([Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism")). [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") are small minorities in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"). About 18% of the population are either agnostic or atheist.[[2]](#cite_note-Census2002-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Crnic-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Radovljica_Brezje_Bazilika_Sv_Vida_28082012_011.jpg/250px-Radovljica_Brezje_Bazilika_Sv_Vida_28082012_011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Radovljica_Brezje_Bazilika_Sv_Vida_28082012_011.jpg)

Basilica of the Virgin Mary in [Brezje](/wiki/Brezje,_Radovljica "Brezje, Radovljica"), also known as the Slovenian National Shrine, is the most visited [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") pilgrimage site in Slovenia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Evangeli%C4%8Danska_cerkev%2C_Bodonci.jpg/250px-Evangeli%C4%8Danska_cerkev%2C_Bodonci.jpg)](/wiki/File:Evangeli%C4%8Danska_cerkev,_Bodonci.jpg)

[Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") church in [Bodonci](/wiki/Bodonci "Bodonci") in the Prekmurje region

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Sts._Cyril_and_Methodius_Church_2.jpg/250px-Sts._Cyril_and_Methodius_Church_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sts._Cyril_and_Methodius_Church_2.jpg)

[Sts. Cyril and Methodius Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Sts._Cyril_and_Methodius_Church_(Ljubljana) "Sts. Cyril and Methodius Church (Ljubljana)") of the [Metropolitanate of Zagreb and Ljubljana](/wiki/Metropolitanate_of_Zagreb_and_Ljubljana "Metropolitanate of Zagreb and Ljubljana")

The dominant **religion in Slovenia** is [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), primarily the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Slovenia "Catholic Church in Slovenia"), which is the largest [Christian denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") in the country. Other Christian groups having significant followings in the country include [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") and [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") ([Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism")). [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") are small minorities in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"). About 18% of the population are either agnostic or atheist.[[2]](#cite_note-Census2002-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Crnic-3)

Religion played a significant role in the development of the  [Slovenian nation](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") and of the country of Slovenia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After a centuries-long tradition of a [state church](/wiki/State_church "State church"), interrupted by the periods of [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") (in the 16th century) and post–World War II [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") (which ousted religion from public life), a degree of separation of the state and the church has been reached in independent Slovenia. In February 2007 Slovenia passed a new Religious Freedom Act with a bias towards the Catholic Church (particularly in regard to state funding) and strict terms for the registration of new religious communities.[[3]](#cite_note-Crnic-3)

## Religion and beliefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Religion and beliefs")]

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Christianity")]

#### Catholicism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Catholicism")]

Main article: [Catholic Church in Slovenia](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Slovenia "Catholic Church in Slovenia")

The [Catholic Church in Slovenia](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Slovenia "Catholic Church in Slovenia") is part of the worldwide [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), under the spiritual leadership of the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome").

There are around 1,135,626 Catholics in the country (about 57.8% of the total population as per the 2002 Census).[[2]](#cite_note-Census2002-2) The country is divided into six [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"), including two [archdioceses](/wiki/Archdiocese "Archdiocese"). The diocese of [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor") was elevated to an archdiocese by Pope [Benedict XVI](/wiki/Benedict_XVI "Benedict XVI") in 2006. Additionally, the pope created three new sees, namely [Novo Mesto](/wiki/Novo_Mesto "Novo Mesto"), [Celje](/wiki/Celje "Celje") and [Murska Sobota](/wiki/Murska_Sobota "Murska Sobota").

#### Protestantism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Protestantism")]

[Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") is tightly-knit with the history of Slovenians, with the Slovenian language having been established in the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"). [Primož Trubar](/wiki/Primo%C5%BE_Trubar "Primož Trubar") was a leading early Slovenian author and a Protestant reformer. He contributed to the development of the [Slovenian language](/wiki/Slovenian_language "Slovenian language") and [Slovenian culture](/wiki/Slovenian_culture "Slovenian culture").

The Reformation flourished in the 16th century, accounting for the vast majority of cultural development in Slovenian. [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") was the most popular Protestant denomination among Slovenians, with minorities, most notably [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism").

Protestantism among Slovenians was aggressively attempted to be wiped out by the [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburgs "Habsburgs") with the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation"). The Counter-Reformation was heavily deployed to the majority of Slovenian-speaking territory. Means used involved murder, extradition, book-burning and a general ban of the Slovenian language. Excluded were eastern regions (such as Prekmurje), ruled by Hungarian nobility, often [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist"). Historically, [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians") had taken up Lutheranism first, before gradually switching to Calvinism. They did not have a policy of extinguishing Lutheranism.

Protestantism among Slovenians survived the Counter-Reformation scattered. Protestantism is a minority group of Christian denominations in the Republic of Slovenia today. The largest community of Protestant Slovenians lives in the [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje") region, most of them are Lutheran.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

#### Eastern Orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodoxy")]

[Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") maintains a significant presence in the country and is practised in majority by Slovenians of [Serbian](/wiki/Serb_people "Serb people") heritage. Eastern Orthodox Christians in Slovenia are under ecclesiastical jurisdiction of the Serbian Orthodox [Metropolitanate of Zagreb and Ljubljana](/wiki/Metropolitanate_of_Zagreb_and_Ljubljana "Metropolitanate of Zagreb and Ljubljana").

Orthodoxy in Slovenian lands has historically been closely associated with the [local Serbian community](/wiki/Serbs_in_Slovenia "Serbs in Slovenia") (particularly [community in White Carniola](/wiki/Serbs_of_White_Carniola "Serbs of White Carniola")).[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5) The first recorded encounters with Orthodoxy in Slovenia occurred during the rule of the [Counts of Celje](/wiki/Counts_of_Celje "Counts of Celje").[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5) In 1434, Count [Ulrich II of Celje](/wiki/Ulrich_II_of_Celje "Ulrich II of Celje") married [Katarina Branković](/wiki/Katarina_Brankovi%C4%87 "Katarina Branković"), daughter of the [Serbian Despot](/wiki/Serbian_Despot "Serbian Despot") [Đurađ Branković](/wiki/%C4%90ura%C4%91_Brankovi%C4%87 "Đurađ Branković"), who brought Orthodox priests to serve at the Celje court.[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5) Orthodox Serbs ([Rascians](/wiki/Rascians "Rascians")) also arrived in Celje’s territories as soldiers.[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5)

During the 15th and 16th centuries, waves of Orthodox migrants, mostly [Vlachs](/wiki/Vlachs "Vlachs") fleeing the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") conquests, settled in what is today Slovenia.[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5) While some assimilated into the local population, others remained in the [White Carniola](/wiki/White_Carniola "White Carniola") region, establishing seven Serbian Uskok settlements, some of which have maintained a continuous Orthodox presence to the present day.[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5)

The region formed part of the [Military Frontier](/wiki/Military_Frontier "Military Frontier"), where Orthodox soldiers were granted land and religious freedom in return for military service.[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5) Following the abolition of the Military Frontier in 1881, three of these villages ([Marindol](/wiki/Marindol "Marindol"), [Milići](/wiki/Mili%C4%87i,_Slovenia "Milići, Slovenia") and [Paunovići](/wiki/Paunovi%C4%87i "Paunovići")) became part of the [Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia "Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia") and later the [Sava Banovina](/wiki/Sava_Banovina "Sava Banovina") in the [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia").[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5) During [World War II in Yugoslavia](/wiki/World_War_II_in_Yugoslavia "World War II in Yugoslavia"), these villages fell under Italian-occupied Slovenia, but after the war, they were transferred back to the [People’s Republic of Croatia](/wiki/People%E2%80%99s_Republic_of_Croatia "People’s Republic of Croatia").[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5) In 1952, the local population voted to re-join the [People’s Republic of Slovenia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Slovenia "Socialist Republic of Slovenia").[[5]](#cite_note-Patafta-5)

### Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Islam")]

Main article: [Islam in Slovenia](/wiki/Islam_in_Slovenia "Islam in Slovenia")

The [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") are ethnically mostly [Bosniaks](/wiki/Bosniaks "Bosniaks") and [ethnic Muslims](/wiki/Muslims_(South-Slavic_ethnic_group) "Muslims (South-Slavic ethnic group)").[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 2014, there were 48,266 [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") in Slovenia, making up about 2.4 percent of the total population.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Muslim community of Slovenia is headed by [Nedžad Grabus](/w/index.php?title=Ned%C5%BEad_Grabus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nedžad Grabus (page does not exist)") [[sl](https://sl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ned%C5%BEad_Grabus "sl:Nedžad Grabus")].[[8]](#cite_note-8)

According to the published data from the 2002 Slovenian census, out of a total of 47,488 Muslims (2.4% of the total population) 2,804 Muslims (5.90% of the total Muslims in Slovenia) declared themselves as ethnic [Slovenian Muslims](/wiki/Slovenian_Muslims "Slovenian Muslims").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

There are also Muslims from Central, South and Southeast Asia, who are not counted in the census because they are migrant workers.

### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Judaism")]

Main article: [History of the Jews in Slovenia](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Slovenia "History of the Jews in Slovenia")

The small [Jewish](/wiki/Jew "Jew") community of [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") ([Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"): *Judovska skupnost Slovenije*) is estimated at 400 to 600 members, with the Jewish community of Slovenia suggesting 500 to 1000 members. Around 130 are officially registered,[[10]](#cite_note-10) most of whom live in the capital, [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana"). The Jewish community was devastated by the [Shoah](/wiki/Shoah "Shoah"), and has never fully recovered. Until 2003, [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana") was the only [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") capital city without a Jewish place of worship.[[11]](#cite_note-Jewish_Virtual_Library_-_Slovenia-11)

### Hinduism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hinduism")]

220 Hindus live in Slovenia, with 70 belonging to the Hindu Religious Community in Slovenia and 150 belonging to the [International Society for Krishna Consciousness](/wiki/International_Society_for_Krishna_Consciousness "International Society for Krishna Consciousness") (Iskcon).[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Atheism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Atheism")]

A 2010 [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") poll found that 26% of the population of Slovenia stated that "I don't believe there is any sort of spirit, God or life force".[[13]](#cite_note-13)
A 2021 World Population Review found that 53% of Slovenians were either non-religious or convinced atheist.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Demographics")]

### Censuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Censuses")]

Religiosity of Slovene citizens according to population censuses 1991, and 2002.

| Religious group | Population  % **1991**[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[3]](#cite_note-Crnic-3) | Population  % **2002**[[2]](#cite_note-Census2002-2) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Christianity | 74.9% | 61.1% |
| *Catholicism* | *71.6%* | 57.8 |
| *Lutheran and other Protestants* | *0.8%* | *0.8%* |
| *Orthodox Christian* | *2.4%* | *2.3%* |
| Islam | 1.5% | 2.4% |
| Other religion | 0.0% | 0.02% |
| Spiritual but not member of religions | 0.2% | 3.5% |
| Atheists | 4.4% | 10.1% |
| Agnostics | - | 0.0% |
| Unknown | 14.6% | 7.1% |
| Not answered | 0.0% | 15.7% |

### Surveys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Surveys")]

* [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") 2012 found about 68% of the population declaring to be Christian, with 64% being members of the Catholic Church. Members of other Christian denominations made up 4% of the population.[[17]](#cite_note-Eurobarometer393-p233-17)
* [International Social Survey Programme](/wiki/International_Social_Survey_Programme "International Social Survey Programme") 2015 found that 64.3% of the population declared to be Christian, with Catholicism being the largest denomination accounting for 62.2% of the respondents, and Eastern Orthodoxy being the second-largest sect comprising 1.5%; members of other Christian denominations made up the 0.6%. A further 34.3% declared to have no religion, and 1.5% declared to belong to other religions.[[18]](#cite_note-ISSP20152-18)

## Religious freedom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Religion_in_Slovenia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Religious freedom")]

Main article: [Freedom of religion in Slovenia](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Slovenia "Freedom of religion in Slovenia")

Slovenia's laws guarantee the freedom of religion and establish a separation between church and state, as well as prohibiting religious discrimination and religious hatred. Religious groups may easily register with the government in order to receive some privileges, largely consisting of various forms of monetary compensation.

Slovenia's laws prohibit circumcision for non-medical reasons and animal slaughtering practices that are necessary for meat to be considered [kosher](/wiki/Kosher "Kosher") or [halal](/wiki/Halal "Halal"). Members of the Jewish and Muslim communities observe these practices outside of the country (importing meat, and traveling to neighboring countries for religious circumcision) without obstruction from Slovenia's government.[[19]](#cite_note-:slov-19)
